words.shortDefinition: Independent components that don't affect each other when changed.
words.simpleDefinition: Independent system components where changing one doesn't break others — such as keeping authentication logic entirely separate from data persistence. This mathematical concept ensures high modularity and extreme separation of concerns.
words.longDefinition: In software architecture, this refers to system components or design decisions that can be changed independently without impacting other parts. Borrowed from mathematics where orthogonal vectors are perpendicular, this principle ensures that a change in one area—like switching from a SQL to a NoSQL database—does not require any modifications to unrelated areas like user interface logic or business rules.
words.connector: Think of orthogonal like Lego blocks — you can swap a red block for a blue one without rebuilding the entire structure.
words.techContext: Used when discussing modular design, separation of concerns, and clean architecture.
words.exampleSentence: Our authentication and authorization systems are orthogonal, so we can swap auth providers without touching permissions.
words.etymology: from Greek orthos ("straight") + gōnia ("angle") — literally "right-angled."

words.shortDefinition: Degree of interdependence between software modules.
words.simpleDefinition: The degree of interdependence between software modules — where low coupling means independence and high coupling results in fragile systems. Tight coupling causes changes in one module to ripple across the entire application.
words.longDefinition: Measures how closely connected different modules or components are within a system. Low coupling means modules are independent and changes in one module minimally affect others, which is ideal for maintainability. Conversely, high coupling creates fragile systems where small changes ripple across boundaries, often leading to 'shotgun surgery' where a single functional change requires modifying dozens of unrelated files across the entire codebase.
words.connector: Think of coupling like train cars — tight coupling is being welded together; loose coupling is using flexible, removable hooks.
words.techContext: Fundamental metric in architecture reviews and refactoring discussions. Usually contrasted with cohesion.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced coupling by introducing interfaces so the payment module doesn't depend directly on Stripe's SDK.
words.etymology: from Old French cople ("bond, link") via Latin copula ("bond, tie").

words.shortDefinition: Degree to which elements within a module belong together.
words.simpleDefinition: The degree to which tasks within a single module are focused and related — high cohesion indicates a module does one thing well. Low cohesion leads to bloated, hard-to-maintain classes.
words.longDefinition: Measures how focused and related the responsibilities within a single module or class are. High cohesion means a module does one thing well and all its internal parts contribute to that single purpose, which makes the code easier to understand and test. Low cohesion indicates a module is performing multiple unrelated tasks, leading to bloated, confusing 'God Objects' that are difficult to reuse.
words.connector: Think of cohesion like a restaurant menu — high cohesion is an Italian place; low cohesion serves sushi, tacos, and BBQ.
words.techContext: Key indicator of module quality. High cohesion with low coupling is the architectural ideal.
words.exampleSentence: The UserService had low cohesion because it handled both authentication and email notifications, so we split it.
words.etymology: from Latin cohaesus, past participle of cohaerere ("to stick together").

words.shortDefinition: Encapsulates data access logic behind a collection-like interface.
words.simpleDefinition: Architectural pattern providing a collection-like interface for data access — hiding the complexity of database queries from business logic. It allows developers to swap persistence layers without modifying the core domain code.
words.longDefinition: Mediates between the domain layer and data mapping layers using a collection-like interface for accessing domain objects. The repository pattern centralizes data access logic and provides a cleaner separation between business logic and persistence. By providing a standardized interface for CRUD operations, it allows the rest of the application to interact with data as if it were an in-memory collection, regardless of the underlying storage.
words.connector: Think of a repository like a library checkout desk — you ask for a title, and the desk handles finding its location.
words.techContext: Common in domain-driven design and layered architectures. Often discussed alongside the Unit of Work pattern.
words.exampleSentence: We use the repository pattern so our business logic doesn't care whether data comes from Postgres or Redis.
words.etymology: from Latin repositorium ("storehouse") from reponere ("to put back, store").

words.shortDefinition: Object model of the domain incorporating both behavior and data.
words.simpleDefinition: Code representation of real-world business concepts combining data and behavior — enabling objects to enforce their own rules, like an Order knowing its own cancellation policy. The logical heart of Domain-Driven Design.
words.longDefinition: A representation of meaningful real-world business concepts in code where domain objects contain both data and the business logic that governs that data. Unlike anemic models that only store values, a true domain model enforces business invariants and encapsulates complex rules. This ensures that the heart of the application remains focused on the problem domain rather than technical implementation details like database schemas or UI frameworks.
words.connector: Think of a domain model like the rules of a board game — they define exactly what the pieces are and how they move.
words.techContext: Central to domain-driven design. Differentiates object-oriented design from procedural or transaction script approaches.
words.exampleSentence: Our domain model handles all business rules, so the Order object knows when it can be canceled or refunded.
words.etymology: domain from Latin dominium ("property, ownership"); model from Latin modulus ("small measure").

words.shortDefinition: Defines application boundaries and coordinates high-level application activity.
words.simpleDefinition: Application layer orchestrating complex workflows — coordinating repositories, email services, and loggers to complete a single user action. It acts as a bridge between the presentation layer and the core domain logic.
words.longDefinition: Establishes a set of available operations and coordinates the application's response in each operation. The service layer encapsulates business logic and provides a well-defined API for clients while delegating core domain logic to domain objects. It acts as an orchestrator, managing transactions, security checks, and cross-cutting concerns to ensure that complex workflows are executed consistently across different parts of the system without duplication.
words.connector: Think of a service layer like an orchestra conductor — coordinating different musicians so they play a single, harmonious piece of music.
words.techContext: Common in layered architectures. Sits between presentation and domain layers to orchestrate workflows.
words.exampleSentence: The service layer coordinates the transaction: it calls the repository, sends the email, and logs the event.
words.etymology: service from Latin servitium ("slavery, servitude"); layer from Old English leger ("bed, stratum").

words.shortDefinition: Layer moving data between objects and database while maintaining independence.
words.simpleDefinition: Independent layer translating between in-memory objects and database rows — keeping the domain model completely isolated from the persistence schema. Allows domain objects to remain ignorant of database implementation details and structure.
words.longDefinition: A layer of software that separates the in-memory domain objects from the database schema. The mapper handles all loading and storing of objects, allowing domain objects to remain completely ignorant of the database structure and implementation details. This isolation is crucial for complex systems where the database schema might be legacy or optimized for performance in ways that don't match the ideal object-oriented domain model.
words.connector: Think of a data mapper like an expert translator — helping two people who speak different languages communicate without them needing to learn.
words.techContext: Contrasts with Active Record pattern. Provides better separation of concerns but requires more boilerplate code.
words.exampleSentence: We use the data mapper pattern so our domain models don't inherit from any ORM base class.
words.etymology: data from Latin datum ("something given"); mapper from Latin mappa ("napkin, cloth").

words.shortDefinition: Pattern where domain objects carry both data and persistence methods.
words.simpleDefinition: Object-oriented pattern where domain objects wrap database rows and carry their own persistence methods — like save() and delete(). It provides a simple, direct way to interact with data but tightly couples domain logic to the database.
words.longDefinition: An object that wraps a row in a database table, encapsulates database access, and adds domain logic on that data. Each instance corresponds to a single row and knows how to save, update, and delete itself directly. While this pattern is very productive for simple CRUD applications and rapid prototyping, it can lead to maintenance challenges in complex systems because it tightly couples business logic to the database schema.
words.connector: Think of active record like a self-checkout item — the product knows its own price and how to pay for itself.
words.techContext: Popular in Rails and other frameworks. Simpler than Data Mapper but couples domain objects to database structure.
words.exampleSentence: Rails uses Active Record, so calling user.save() persists the object directly to the database.
words.etymology: active from Latin activus ("practical, operative"); record from Latin recordari ("to remember").

words.shortDefinition: Maintains list of affected objects and coordinates atomic changes.
words.simpleDefinition: Management pattern tracking all objects affected by a business transaction — coordinating final database updates as a single, atomic operation. It ensures all changes succeed or fail together, maintaining strict data consistency.
words.longDefinition: Tracks all changes made during a business transaction and writes them out as a single unit at the end of the operation. It handles the correct ordering of database operations, manages concurrency conflicts, and ensures that all changes succeed or fail together as an atomic action. This pattern minimizes database round-trips and prevents the system from entering an inconsistent state by grouping multiple related updates together.
words.connector: Think of a unit of work like a shopping cart — you gather all your items first, then pay for everything in one transaction.
words.techContext: Essential for transactional consistency. Common in ORMs like Entity Framework and SQLAlchemy.
words.exampleSentence: The unit of work batches all our changes so we only hit the database once at commit time.
words.etymology: unit from Latin unitas ("oneness"); work from Old English weorc ("deed, action").

words.shortDefinition: Defers loading an object's data until it is actually accessed.
words.simpleDefinition: Performance pattern deferring data retrieval until an object is actually accessed — rather than loading everything upfront. This significantly reduces initial load times and memory usage for complex, highly connected data structures.
words.longDefinition: An object that doesn't contain all of the data you need but knows how to get it when required. This pattern reduces initial load time and memory footprint by fetching related data only when it is actually accessed. However, architects must be careful to avoid the N+1 query problem, where accessing a collection of lazy-loaded objects triggers a separate database call for every single item.
words.connector: Think of lazy load like opening apps on your phone — they don't all start at once; they only launch when you tap them.
words.techContext: Common in ORMs for loading related entities. Can cause N+1 query problems if not managed carefully.
words.exampleSentence: We lazy load the user's order history because most requests don't need it.
words.etymology: lazy from Middle Low German lasich ("languid, weak"); load from Old English lad ("way, course").

words.shortDefinition: Simple object used to carry data between processes or layers.
words.simpleDefinition: Simple object used specifically to carry data between processes or layers — containing no business logic. DTOs decouple internal domain models from external API contracts, preventing breaking changes for clients when internal schemas evolve.
words.longDefinition: An object that carries data between processes to reduce the number of remote method calls. DTOs are simple data containers with no business logic, often used to aggregate data for transfer across network or process boundaries. By using DTOs, developers can define strict, stable API contracts that remain unchanged even if the internal domain models or database schemas evolve significantly over time, improving system resilience.
words.connector: Think of a DTO like a plain shipping box — it's an empty container used just to move items from one warehouse to another.
words.techContext: Common in distributed systems and API design. Decouples external contracts from internal domain models.
words.exampleSentence: We return DTOs from our API so internal model changes don't break client contracts.
words.etymology: acronym for Data Transfer Object, popularized by Martin Fowler in Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (2002).

words.shortDefinition: Encapsulates access to an external system behind a simplified interface.
words.simpleDefinition: Object encapsulating access to external systems — providing a simplified interface while hiding complex networking or protocol details. Gateways act as a protective barrier, making external dependencies easier to mock and test in isolation.
words.longDefinition: Wraps the API of an external system with an interface that is easier to use or more suitable for your application. It isolates the complexity of external communication—handling protocols, serialization, and error mapping—and provides a stable point for testing and substitution. Using a gateway allows the application to depend on a local interface rather than being tightly coupled to the specific quirks of a third-party service.
words.connector: Think of a gateway like a hotel concierge — they handle all the complex outside arrangements so you only have to ask one person.
words.techContext: Used to wrap third-party APIs, legacy systems, or external services. Enables easier testing via mocking.
words.exampleSentence: We built a payment gateway to abstract away the differences between Stripe and PayPal.
words.etymology: from gate (Old English geat) + way (Old English weg) — literally "a way through a gate."

words.shortDefinition: Immutable object whose equality depends on property values, not identity.
words.simpleDefinition: Small immutable object representing a descriptive aspect of the domain — defined by its values rather than a unique identity. Two instances are considered identical if all their fields match exactly, preventing identity-based bugs.
words.longDefinition: A small object that represents a descriptive aspect of the domain with no conceptual identity of its own. Two value objects are considered equal if their property values are equal, regardless of where they are stored in memory. They are always immutable to avoid aliasing bugs, ensuring that a change to a value in one part of the system doesn't unexpectedly impact other parts that reference it.
words.connector: Think of a value object like a $5 bill — you only care about the value it represents, not which specific physical bill you hold.
words.techContext: Core concept in domain-driven design. Common examples include Money, DateRange, Address, or EmailAddress.
words.exampleSentence: We made Email a value object so two instances with the same address are considered equal.
words.etymology: value from Latin valere ("to be strong, be worth"); object from Latin obiectus ("thing thrown before").

words.shortDefinition: Logical boundary where a specific domain model and language are applicable.
words.simpleDefinition: Explicit linguistic and logical boundary in Domain-Driven Design — within which a specific model is strictly defined. Terms like 'User' might have entirely different meanings and properties when moving from one context to another.
words.longDefinition: A central pattern in domain-driven design that defines the explicit limits within which a specific domain model is applicable. Within a bounded context, terms have specific, unambiguous meanings and the model is internally consistent. This prevents confusion in large organizations where the same word, such as 'Account,' might refer to a login credential in one department but a financial record in another department.
words.connector: Think of a bounded context like country borders — 'football' means soccer in the UK, but American football once you cross into the US.
words.techContext: Critical for microservices architecture and team organization. Helps manage complexity in large systems.
words.exampleSentence: In the billing context, 'user' means a paying customer, but in the support context, it includes free trial accounts.
words.etymology: bounded from Old French bonde ("limit"); context from Latin contextus ("a joining together").

codeExample.code:
# Billing Context:
User:
  - subscriptionId
  - paymentMethod

# Support Context:
User:
  - ticketHistory
  - supportTier

words.shortDefinition: Cluster of domain objects treated as a single transactional unit.
words.simpleDefinition: Cluster of domain objects treated as a single unit for data consistency — protected by a designated root entity. External code can only interact with the root, ensuring all internal business invariants are always maintained.
words.longDefinition: A pattern in domain-driven design where a cluster of related objects is treated as a single unit for data changes. Every aggregate has a designated root entity that acts as the only entry point for external interaction. This structure ensures that all business invariants and consistency rules are strictly enforced within the cluster, as no external code can modify the internal components of the aggregate directly.
words.connector: Think of an aggregate like a car — you drive and maintain the car (root), without needing to manage the internal engine parts individually.
words.techContext: Defines transactional boundaries in domain models. Each aggregate has exactly one root entity.
words.exampleSentence: The Order aggregate includes OrderItems and ShippingAddress, but external code only touches Order directly.
words.etymology: from Latin aggregatus, past participle of aggregare ("to add to a flock") from ad- + grex ("flock").

words.shortDefinition: Domain model where objects contain data but lack business logic.
words.simpleDefinition: Domain-Driven Design anti-pattern where objects contain data but lack all business logic and behavior. This forces logic into separate service classes, violating basic object-oriented principles by completely separating data from the rules that govern it.
words.longDefinition: An anti-pattern where domain objects are just data containers with getters and setters, while all business logic lives in separate service classes. This contradicts fundamental object-oriented principles by separating data from the behavior that operates on it. Over time, anemic models lead to procedural codebases where business rules are scattered across many services, making the system harder to maintain, test, and evolve as it grows.
words.connector: Think of an anemic model like a mannequin — it looks like a person but it has no life and can't do anything itself.
words.techContext: Considered an anti-pattern by domain-driven design advocates, though sometimes pragmatic for simple CRUD applications.
words.exampleSentence: Our domain model was anemic—just POJOs with getters—so all the business rules ended up scattered across service classes.
words.etymology: anemic from Greek anaimia ("lack of blood") from an- ("without") + haima ("blood"); model from Latin modulus.

words.shortDefinition: System organization split into horizontal tiers with downward-only dependencies.
words.simpleDefinition: System organization split into horizontal functional tiers — where each layer communicates only with the one directly beneath it. This promotes a strict separation of concerns, typically dividing UI, business logic, and persistence logic.
words.longDefinition: Divides software into horizontal layers such as presentation, application, domain, and infrastructure. Each layer has a specific, well-defined role and depends only on the layers directly beneath it. This promotes a strict separation of concerns, making the system easier to manage and test. However, developers must be careful to avoid 'leaky abstractions' where technical details from the persistence layer accidentally bleed into the UI or business logic.
words.connector: Think of layered architecture like the floors of a building — the foundation supports the lobby, which supports the offices above.
words.techContext: Classic enterprise architecture pattern. Often implemented as MVC, three-tier, or n-tier architectures.
words.exampleSentence: We use a layered architecture: the API layer calls the service layer, which calls repositories for data access.
words.etymology: layer from Old English leger ("bed, stratum"); architecture from Greek arkhitekton ("master builder").

codeExample.code:
# Typical layers:
presentation/    # UI, controllers
application/     # use cases, orchestration
domain/          # business logic
infrastructure/  # DB, external services

words.shortDefinition: Isolates core business logic from external concerns via interchangeable ports.
words.simpleDefinition: Architecture pattern isolating core business logic from external frameworks, databases, and UIs using 'ports' and 'adapters.' This allows the central application to remain completely unchanged even when external technical implementations are swapped.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Ports and Adapters, this pattern places the core business logic at the center, isolated from external technical concerns. Ports define the interfaces for required services, while adapters implement those interfaces for specific technologies like databases, message brokers, or web UIs. This allows the core application to remain unchanged even as technical requirements evolve, enabling easy testing and the ability to swap out external dependencies.
words.connector: Think of hexagonal like a universal socket — different chargers (adapters) can plug into the same port to power the central device.
words.techContext: Popular in domain-driven design. Makes testing easier by allowing mock adapters for external dependencies.
words.exampleSentence: Hexagonal architecture let us run the same core logic with both a web UI and a CLI by swapping adapters.
words.etymology: hexagonal from Greek hex ("six") + gōnia ("angle"); coined by Alistair Cockburn in 2005.

words.shortDefinition: Design principle of dividing a program into sections, each addressing one concern.
words.simpleDefinition: Design principle of dividing a computer program into distinct sections — each addressing a specific, separate concern. It reduces overall system complexity by ensuring that changes in one area do not affect unrelated areas of logic.
words.longDefinition: Separation of concerns is a fundamental design principle for managing complexity in software systems. By breaking a program into distinct modules that each handle a specific responsibility, developers can reduce coupling and increase cohesion. This allows individual components to be developed, tested, and updated independently without affecting the rest of the system. Common examples include separating the user interface from the business logic or the data storage layer, leading to more modular and maintainable architectures.
words.connector: Think of separation of concerns like a restaurant kitchen — the chef cooks while the dishwasher cleans, so they don't get in the way.
words.techContext: Foundational principle. When you see a function handling HTTP, business logic, and database access, that's a separation of concerns violation.
words.exampleSentence: We achieved separation of concerns by splitting the controller (HTTP), service (business logic), and repository (data access) into distinct modules.
words.etymology: coined by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1974 paper 'On the role of scientific thought.'

words.shortDefinition: Technique where objects receive required dependencies from external sources rather than creating them.
words.simpleDefinition: Design pattern where objects receive their required dependencies from the outside rather than creating them internally. This technique promotes loose coupling and allows for easy swapping of components, which is especially beneficial during automated testing.
words.longDefinition: A form of inversion of control where dependencies are provided to a component by an external assembler rather than the component creating or finding them itself. This promotes loose coupling, as classes no longer need to know how to instantiate their collaborators. It also makes automated testing significantly easier, as developers can easily 'inject' mock versions of expensive or complex dependencies like databases and third-party APIs.
words.connector: Think of dependency injection like a restaurant — you don't build the kitchen yourself; the owner provides the equipment you need to work.
words.techContext: Core pattern in Spring, Angular, and modern frameworks. Enables constructor injection, setter injection, or interface injection.
words.exampleSentence: We use dependency injection so our tests can pass in a mock database instead of hitting the real one.
words.etymology: dependency from Latin dependere ("to hang from"); injection from Latin iniectio ("a throwing in").

words.shortDefinition: Principle where a framework manages flow by calling application code.
words.simpleDefinition: Design principle where the overall flow of control is managed by a framework rather than the custom application code. Instead of the application calling library functions, the framework calls into the application at specific points.
words.longDefinition: A design principle where the flow of control is inverted compared to traditional procedural programming. Instead of your custom application code calling library functions to perform tasks, a framework manages the overall program flow and calls into your code at specific hooks or extension points. This allows the framework to handle common boilerplate tasks like request routing or resource management, letting developers focus purely on business logic.
words.connector: Think of inversion of control like a talent show — you wait for the host (framework) to call your name before performing.
words.techContext: Fundamental to modern frameworks like Spring, React, and Express. Often implemented via callbacks, events, or dependency injection.
words.exampleSentence: React uses inversion of control—you define components and React decides when to render them.
words.etymology: inversion from Latin inversio ("a turning upside down"); control from Medieval Latin contrarotulum ("register").

words.shortDefinition: Simplified interface providing high-level access to a complex underlying subsystem.
words.simpleDefinition: Design pattern providing a unified, simplified interface to a large and complex subsystem. It hides the messy details of multiple internal classes and external dependencies, exposing only the high-level actions required by the client code.
words.longDefinition: Provides a unified, simplified interface to a large and complex set of interfaces in a subsystem. By defining a higher-level interface, the facade makes the subsystem easier to use and reduces the cognitive load on developers who only need a subset of the available functionality. It also decouples the client code from the internal implementation details of the subsystem, allowing those details to change without impacting clients.
words.connector: Think of a facade like a hotel concierge — you ask for one thing and they handle the dozen complex steps to arrange it.
words.techContext: Common when wrapping legacy code or complex libraries. Reduces coupling between subsystems and client code.
words.exampleSentence: We built a facade over the AWS SDK so our application code doesn't need to know about all the S3, SQS, and Lambda details.
words.etymology: from French façade ("front of a building") from Italian facciata, from faccia ("face").

words.shortDefinition: Structural pattern converting an existing interface into another expected interface.
words.simpleDefinition: Structural pattern converting the interface of one class into another interface expected by a client. It acts as a translator, allowing classes with incompatible interfaces to work together seamlessly without modifying their original source code.
words.longDefinition: Allows classes with incompatible interfaces to work together by wrapping one interface to match another. It acts as a translator between two different interfaces, enabling the reuse of existing code or third-party libraries without modifying their source. This pattern is particularly useful when integrating legacy systems into modern architectures or when a client expects a specific interface that a vendor-provided class does not implement out of the box.
words.connector: Think of an adapter like a travel plug — it lets your foreign device plug into a wall socket with a different shape.
words.techContext: Common when integrating third-party libraries or legacy code. Also called a wrapper pattern.
words.exampleSentence: We wrote an adapter to make the old authentication system work with our new user service interface.
words.etymology: from Latin adaptare ("to fit, adjust") from ad- ("to") + aptare ("to fit").

words.shortDefinition: Behavioral pattern defining a family of interchangeable algorithms at runtime.
words.simpleDefinition: Behavioral pattern defining a group of algorithms and making them interchangeable at runtime through a common interface. It allows the specific execution logic to vary independently from the clients that use it, effectively replacing complex conditionals.
words.longDefinition: Encapsulates a family of algorithms or business rules into separate classes with a common interface, allowing them to vary independently from the clients that use them. Instead of using large, nested conditional blocks to choose between different behaviors, a client can select the appropriate strategy at runtime. This promotes the Open/Closed Principle, as new behaviors can be added by creating new strategy classes without modifying the existing code.
words.connector: Think of the strategy pattern like a camera's shooting modes — the camera stays the same, but you swap the settings logic for portraits or sports.
words.techContext: Useful for replacing conditional logic with polymorphism. Common in payment processing, sorting, or validation scenarios.
words.exampleSentence: We use the strategy pattern for shipping calculations—domestic, international, and express are different strategy implementations.
words.etymology: from Greek strategia ("generalship, office of a general") from strategos ("general").

words.shortDefinition: Communication pattern where objects subscribe to receive automatic state notifications.
words.simpleDefinition: Communication pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them automatically of any state changes. This promotes extremely loose coupling by allowing disparate modules to react to events without direct knowledge of the event source.
words.longDefinition: Defines a one-to-many dependency where when one object changes state, all its registered dependents are notified and updated automatically. This pattern promotes loose coupling because the subject doesn't need to know the specific identities or implementations of its observers. It is a cornerstone of event-driven architectures, allowing disparate parts of a system to react to state changes in a flexible, decoupled, and highly scalable manner.
words.connector: Think of the observer pattern like a YouTube subscription — you get notified automatically the moment a creator publishes a new video.
words.techContext: Foundation of event-driven architectures and reactive programming. Implemented in pub/sub systems, event emitters, and reactive streams.
words.exampleSentence: Our notification system uses the observer pattern—when an order ships, multiple observers handle email, SMS, and webhook notifications.
words.etymology: from Latin observare ("to watch, note, heed") from ob- ("before") + servare ("to keep, watch").
